mainmatter.p: By the univalence principle for functions (), we may consider just the case that f \equiv g and H \equiv 1_{ f }, and so we must show that \mathsf {refl} \cdot \mathsf {ap}_{ g } p = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } p \cdot \mathsf {refl}. By identification induction, we may assume x \equiv y and p \equiv \mathsf {refl} to show \mathsf {refl} \cdot \mathsf {refl} = \mathsf {refl} \cdot \mathsf {refl}, which is immediate.

Let H \colon f \sim g be a homotopy between two functions f,g \colon A \to B. For any identification p \colon x =y in A, we have an identification H x \cdot \mathsf {ap}_{ g } \, p = \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \, p \cdot Hy.

frontmatter.title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

mainmatter.p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } {Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.
